name: Mendelevium
name_origin: Named in honor of Dmitri Mendeleev, the Russian chemist who developed the periodic table.

discovery.discoverer: Albert Ghiorso, Bernard Harvey, Gregory Choppin, Stanley Thompson, Glenn Seaborg

occurrence.abundance_summary: Synthetic element not found in nature; produced by bombarding einsteinium-253 targets with helium-4 (alpha) particles in particle accelerators, typically a few atoms at a time.

compounds.name: Mendelevium(III) chloride
compounds.summary: Inferred chloride characterized only by tracer-scale ion-exchange experiments; no bulk samples have been prepared.

uses: Fundamental research into transactinide chemistry, particularly the relative stability of +2 and +3 oxidation states in heavy actinides
Studies of relativistic effects on actinide electronic structure
reactivity_summary: Bulk mendelevium has never been isolated. From tracer experiments, mendelevium shows an unusually accessible +2 oxidation state for an actinide, attributed to the stability of the half-filled and full f-shell configurations; the +3 state is also observed.
isotopes_summary: All isotopes are radioactive. Mendelevium-258 has the longest half-life (51.5 days). Md-256 (half-life 1.27 hours) was the first isotope discovered and is still commonly produced in accelerator experiments.

citations.source: IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements
Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory - Mendelevium discovery history
